metrics.reason: Current state of the failing Web Threat Defense User Service; confirms it is stopped.
Microsoft Defender service; a dependency for the web threat service. If stopped, it can cause the user service to stop.
Base Filtering Engine – required for network filtering used by Defender. Its failure often leads to Defender services stopping.
Windows Firewall service; interacts with BFE and Defender. Issues here may cascade to the web threat service.
Remote Procedure Call service; many Windows services depend on it. Its failure can cause other services to stop unexpectedly.
Windows Event Log service; if unavailable, services may fail to start or report errors.
Time service; time sync problems can affect service start‑up logic.
Network client service; required for any network communication the web threat service may perform.
Push Notification service; sometimes used by security agents for cloud communication.
Monitoring agent itself; ensures that metric collection is still functional for further analysis.
Overall CPU utilization; high CPU can prevent services from starting or cause them to crash.
User‑mode CPU time; spikes may indicate workload that starves the service.
Kernel‑mode CPU time; high values can affect service stability.
Deferred Procedure Call time; excessive DPC can indicate driver/network issues impacting services.
Interrupt handling time; high interrupt load can degrade service performance.
Rate of context switches; very high rates may signal CPU contention affecting service threads.
Number of threads waiting for CPU; a sustained high queue length indicates CPU bottleneck.
Rate of memory pages being read/written; high paging can cause service latency or failure.
Page fault rate; elevated faults suggest memory pressure that could stop services.
Availability of system page table entries; low values can prevent new processes from starting.
Size of the system cache; helps assess overall memory health.
Non‑paged pool usage; exhaustion can cause driver/service failures.
Paging file utilization; high usage indicates memory pressure that may force services to stop.
Disk idle time on C:; low idle time signals heavy I/O which can delay service start‑up.
Average read latency on C:; high latency can affect services that read configuration files.
Average write latency on C:; relevant for services that write logs or state.
Number of pending I/O operations on C:; a long queue indicates I/O bottleneck.
Read IOPS on C:; helps correlate I/O load with service behavior.
Write IOPS on C:; same rationale as reads.
Disk idle time on D:; if the service stores data on D:, this metric is relevant.
Free space on C: drive; insufficient space can prevent service from writing logs or temp files.
Percentage of used space on C:; helps quickly spot low‑disk‑space conditions.
Free space on D: drive; relevant if the service uses D: for data.
Available swap; low swap can indicate memory exhaustion.
Host uptime; a recent reboot may explain why a service is not running.
Confirms the Zabbix agent is reachable; ensures collected metrics are reliable.
Network receive errors; network issues can affect services that communicate with cloud endpoints.
Network transmit errors; same relevance as above.
